original_question: How is the development of each business segment of Meta in 2025?
final_answer: In 2025, Meta's Family of Apps segment functions as a highly profitable growth engine by leveraging AI to optimize ad pricing and impressions, while Reality Labs operates as a strategic cost center where widening losses reflect a massive capital commitment to building the next generation of compute infrastructure and immersive hardware. This conclusion emerges from a synthesis of the analyses across the Advertising Monetization & Measurement, AI, Compute Infrastructure & Innovation Approach, and Financial Performance, Capital Allocation & Liquidity pillars.

pillar_analysis.paragraph: In terms of Advertising Monetization & Measurement, Meta continues to report its financial performance through its two core segments, Family of Apps (FoA) and Reality Labs (RL) (IND_1). The Family of Apps segment demonstrated strong development in 2025, generating $198,759 million in total revenue, which represents a 22% increase compared to the previous year (IND_8). Within this segment, advertising revenue alone reached $196,175 million, underscoring its role as the primary engine for monetization (IND_14). This growth was fundamentally driven by a 12% year-over-year increase in ad impressions and a 9% rise in the average price per ad (IND_7). Geographically, the increase in ad impressions was particularly significant in the Asia-Pacific region, fueled by rising user engagement (IND_7). Meta attributes the increase in average ad pricing to heightened advertiser demand resulting from continuous improvements in ad targeting and measurement tools (IND_9). However, the company noted that while Reels usage is growing, it continues to monetize at a lower rate than Feed and Stories products (IND_5). Despite these monetization gaps in newer formats, the FoA segment maintained a robust operating margin of 52% in 2025 (IND_11). To sustain this momentum, Meta is heavily investing in AI-driven infrastructure, contributing to a total research and development spend of $57,372 million (IND_12). Furthermore, the company is diversifying its monetization through FoA other revenue, which grew 50% in 2025 due to the expansion of WhatsApp paid messaging and Meta Verified (IND_2).

pillar_analysis.paragraph: In terms of AI, Compute Infrastructure & Innovation Approach, Meta operates through two reportable segments, Family of Apps (FoA) and Reality Labs (RL), to execute its long-term technology roadmap (IND_1). The company’s cost structure in 2025 heavily favored the core business, with 82% of total costs and expenses recognized in FoA compared to 18% in RL (IND_2). Central to this development is a massive scaling of technical infrastructure, as AI initiatives across the business continue to drive substantial costs in hardware and specialized headcount (IND_5). This infrastructure push is evident in the growth of servers and network assets to $98.04 billion, alongside a rise in construction in progress to $50.52 billion (IND_13). Consequently, research and development expenses surged by 31% to $57.37 billion, primarily to support generative AI and superintelligence projects (IND_11). To maintain this momentum, Meta has secured $131.05 billion in non-cancelable contractual commitments, largely for third-party cloud capacity and server infrastructure (IND_15). These investments align with a strategic vision to transition from 2D screens toward an immersive ecosystem of devices and AI-powered experiences (IND_6). For the upcoming year, the company anticipates a further ramp-up in capital expenditures, projecting between $115 billion and $135 billion for 2026 to support its AI efforts and core operations (IND_10).

pillar_analysis.paragraph: In terms of Financial Performance, Capital Allocation & Liquidity, Meta’s total revenue for the full year 2025 reached $200,966 million, reflecting a 22% growth rate (IND_1). Family of Apps revenue grew by over $36 billion year-over-year, remaining the primary driver of top-line expansion (IND_4). This segment maintained high profitability with an operating margin of 52%, although this was a slight decrease from the 54% reported in 2024 (IND_11). Reality Labs revenue saw a marginal increase of 3%, yet the segment's operating loss widened to $19,193 million (IND_2). Significant investment in the future was reflected in research and development costs, which rose to 29% of total revenue (IND_13). Total consolidated costs and expenses for the period grew to $117,690 million, driven by higher compensation and infrastructure needs (IND_12). Management continued its aggressive capital return program, utilizing $26.26 billion for share repurchases throughout 2025 (IND_9). Shareholders also benefited from a 5% increase in the quarterly cash dividend to $0.525 per share (IND_10). The company ended 2025 with a strong liquidity position, holding $81.59 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities (IND_7). Revenue from advertising sources alone accounted for $196,175 million of the total (IND_14). Growth was distributed across all geographic regions, with the Asia-Pacific market contributing $53,817 million (IND_15).
